abstract:
Policy advisory systems, tasked with delivering evidence-informed guidance to governments, face acute pressure during periods of crisis. Such high-stakes environments often trigger conflicting interpretations of how to respond, tensions that are only exacerbated by inherent knowledge gaps and the unpredictable trajectory of unfolding events. Whether navigating financial instability, political upheaval, global pandemics, or national security threats, governments must operate under constraints that are frequently beyond their control. These decision-making burdens were particularly visible during the COVID-19 pandemic, where advisory responses diverged significantly across different national contexts.

This introductory article frames a collection of research that offers new perspectives on how governmental policy advisory systems navigated the COVID-19 crisis. In many instances, advice had to be formulated amidst fierce public debate over how to balance public health outcomes with economic stability and social cohesion. The studies presented here scrutinize these advisory systems by examining the quality of available evidence, the disciplinary diversity of expert bodies, and the evolving roles of experts—whether acting as trusted internal advisors or external critics. Furthermore, the contributors explore the distinction between experts as neutral providers of data versus active "shapers" of knowledge, as well as the varying degrees of formality in their interactions with political leaders. By extending these inquiries to "creeping crises" like climate change, this special issue advances our understanding of how expert knowledge informs crisis governance and elucidates the complex, shifting relationship between policy advisors and political decision-makers in turbulent times.
